policy_statement: Enact legislation to include climate mitigation, adaptation/resilience, and biodiversity as potential topics in comprehensive plans.
mechanism_description: The State enacts legislation so comprehensive plans can consider climate mitigation, adaptation and resilience, and biodiversity, strengthening planning integration and institutional capacity to act on climate risks.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance/planning mainstreaming → ↑ institutional adaptive capacity → climate-informed decisions → ↓ exposure/vulnerability to climate hazards
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Statutory/administrative set for comprehensive planning are expanded so planners must/are permitted to integrate climate mitigation, adaptation/resilience, and biodiversity considerations into long-term plans and associated decision processes.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Improved risk assessment and cross-sector coordination changes where and how development and infrastructure are sited, designed, and maintained (e.g., avoiding floodplains, incorporating heat/flood standards, protecting critical services), thereby altering the interaction between climate hazards and the built environment.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced impacts from climate hazards (lower exposure, sensitivity, and service disruption), i.e., increased resilience/adaptive capacity; mitigation co-benefits may occur but are not the dominant guaranteed outcome of the mechanism.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved, climate-informed land-use and capital planning that reduces future exposure and vulnerability to climate hazards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s core effect is mainstreaming climate considerations into comprehensive plans, which primarily builds institutional capacity and drives hazard-informed land-use/infrastructure decisions → reduced climate risk.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While plans may also include mitigation and biodiversity actions, the mechanism described (institutional capacity via planning integration) most reliably and broadly produces adaptation outcomes (risk reduction) rather than direct emissions reductions or efficiency gains.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a jurisdiction uses comprehensive-plan mainstreaming mainly to mandate emissions targets or energy policies, mitigation could become dominant—but that is not inherent to this canonical mechanism focused on adaptive capacity/resilience integration.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that enables/requires climate mainstreaming in comprehensive planning to strengthen institutional adaptive capacity will chiefly function as an adaptation capacity-building lever, even when it also enables mitigation or nature elements.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer assignment (e.g., “governance capacity-building,” “risk-informed land-use,” “ecosystem-based adaptation”) depends on the specific plan requirements and implementation evidence at the policy level and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some comprehensive-plan climate mainstreaming statutes can be written in a way that prioritizes mitigation (e.g., enforceable GHG caps) or biodiversity/carbon sinks; however, the canonical mechanism here emphasizes institutional adaptive capacity and resilience, making Adaptation the best primary classification.
key_indicators: "enact legislation"; "amending relevant statutes"; "include consideration of climate mitigation, adaptation and resilience, and biodiversity"; "topics in comprehensive plans"
co_benefits: Mitigation and biodiversity conservation benefits may occur via climate/biodiversity-informed planning decisions, but are not specified as quantified or binding outcomes beyond inclusion in plans.
instance_edge_case_notes: The text says "include … as potential topics," which could imply permissive authority rather than a strict requirement; classification remains a regulatory mandate because it is a statutory change governing planning content, but the strength of the mandate may vary by how the amendment is drafted.
